figures.caption: Table 5: Experimental results of reinforcement learning on Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. We report the average performance for 16 runs on AIME24 and AIME25, and 4 runs on the others, as well as the improvement of AIPO over LUFFY. We abbreviate LMB as LiveMathBench v202505, LCB as LiveCodeBench v6, and RG as Reasoning Gym. ♠ denotes the in-domain evaluation benchmark and ♣ denotes the out-of-domain benchmark. The RL performance of Llama is provided in § C.2.

Table 6: Experimental results of AIPO with Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct. We report the average performance for 16 runs on AIME24 and AIME25, and 4 runs on others. We abbreviate LMB as LiveMathBench v202505, LCB as LiveCodeBench v6, and RG as Reasoning Gym.

Figure 1: Comparison between existing methods and the proposed AIPO. (A) Motivation: current RLVR suffers from restricted exploration and is bounded by pretrained LLM capability. (B) Conventional approaches rely on expert trajectories from superior teacher LLMs to enhance reasoning. (C) We propose to expand the reasoning boundary via active interaction.

Table 1: Experimental results of AIPO and baselines with Qwen and Llama, where LUFFY and AIPO are all based on GRPO. We report the average performance for 16 runs on AIME24 and AIME25, and 4 runs on the others, as well as the improvement of AIPO over LUFFY. We abbreviate LMB as LiveMathBench v202505, LCB as LiveCodeBench v6, and RG as Reasoning Gym. ♠ denotes the in-domain evaluation benchmark and ♣ denotes the out-of-domain benchmark. The RL performance of Llama is provided in § C.2.

Table 2: Ablation study of AIPO’s components w.r.t. the modified importance sampling coefficient (abbreviated as IS, Equation (5)) and clipping strategy (abbreviated as CS, Equation (6)). We also include the results where we masked the external tokens from external policy models.
Figure 4: Capability Boundary Expansion of AIPO measured by the Pass@n metric.

Figure 2: Illustration of AIPO. In the AIPO framework, during each rollout, the policy model engages in active interactions with collaborators. We then compute the reward and optimize the policy model using losses derived from both internal (on-policy) and external (off-policy) tokens. Additionally, we propose an amended importance sampling coefficient and clipping strategy to mitigate off-policy errors and the vanishing gradient problem for external tokens.

Table 8: Experimental results of AIPO and baselines with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B. We report the average performance for 16 runs on AIME24 and AIME25, and 4 runs on others. We abbreviate LMB as LiveMathBench v202505, LCB as LiveCodeBench v6, and RG as Reasoning Gym.
Figure 5: Training Dynamics of AIPO and baselines on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with the same model as collaborators.
